title: SEGMENT 8 — Transcription: the provirus as a cellular gene

lines.text: SEGMENT 8 — Transcription: the provirus as a cellular gene
Once integrated, the provirus is, from the perspective of host machinery, just another stretch of chromosomal DNA.
It will be transcribed by host RNA polymerase II, packaged into chromatin, regulated by host transcription factors.
There is no viral RNA polymerase in a retrovirus.
Every viral RNA that ever gets made is made by your own RNA pol II reading your own chromosomes.
That has profound consequences.
It means that transcription of the provirus is regulated like a cellular gene, with all the strengths and weaknesses that brings.
It means that latency — a state where the provirus is integrated but transcriptionally silent — is a fundamental property of every retrovirus, because chromatin can be inactive.
It means that the LTR is, structurally, a promoter, and we can analyze it like any other promoter.
The transcription start site sits at the boundary between U3 and R in the 5' LTR.
U3 contains the promoter and enhancer.
For HIV-1, U3 contains:
A TATA box about 25 base pairs upstream of the start, which positions RNA pol II.
Three Sp1 binding sites, an upstream constitutive transcription factor binding region.
Two NF-κB binding sites, which are critical — NF-κB is the transcription factor that gets activated when a T cell is stimulated through its T-cell receptor or by cytokines like TNF-α.

So when the cell carrying a latent HIV provirus gets activated as part of a normal immune response, NF-κB translocates to the nucleus, binds those LTR sites, and turns on transcription of the provirus.
This is one of the central reasons HIV preferentially replicates in activated T cells: the virus has built its promoter to be turned on by the same signals that turn the cell on.
Various other sites: NFAT, AP-1, TCF/LEF, depending on cell type.
So transcription initiates.
Pol II loads.
It starts to elongate.
And immediately runs into a problem.
There is a structured RNA element near the very beginning of every HIV-1 transcript called TAR, the trans-activation response element.
TAR is a stable RNA hairpin that forms in the nascent transcript almost as soon as it's made.
TAR acts as a pause site — Pol II, having transcribed about fifty nucleotides, falls off or pauses.
In the absence of any countermeasure, transcription terminates here.
Only short abortive transcripts are made.
The cell has effectively shut down expression.
The countermeasure is a viral protein called Tat.
Tat is encoded by HIV.
It binds TAR.
It recruits a host elongation factor called P-TEFb — positive transcription elongation factor b — which is a complex of cyclin T1 and CDK9.
CDK9 phosphorylates the C-terminal domain of Pol II, kicks it out of pause, and turns it into an elongation-competent polymerase.
The net effect is that Tat is essential for productive transcription of the HIV provirus.
Without Tat you get pause and abort.
With Tat you get full-length transcripts.
This is a marvelous example of a virus exploiting host machinery.
Pol II pausing is a normal regulatory mechanism for cellular genes; HIV has installed an RNA element and a viral factor that releases that pause specifically for its own promoter.
Why is this elegant arrangement important to know?
Two reasons.

First, the Tat-TAR axis has been a drug-development target for decades, with compounds like didehydro-cortistatin A that can lock the virus in a deeply silent state — the "block-and-lock" approach we'll cover in segment 18.
Second, Tat-deficient proviruses are profoundly silenced, which is one of the reasons HIV latency is so hard to break.

The full-length RNA produced by Tat-stimulated transcription is the primary transcript — it runs from R in the 5' LTR all the way to R in the 3' LTR, with a 5' cap, internal splice sites, and a 3' poly-A.
That single transcript will be processed into multiple different mRNAs and will also serve as the genomic RNA for new virions.
How it gets sorted into all those fates is the next segment.
